summary.brief: Cockayne v. Bristol Hospital, Inc.. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Bruce Cockayne was admitted to the defendant on February 11, 2014, and, during this admission, he received treatments of a medication administered rectally via enema.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On February 11, 2014, Bruce Cockayne was readmitted to the defendant after fainting, likely due to continued diarrhea and the resulting loss of fluids.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On the morning of February 14, 2014, Bruce Cockayne suffered a 'massive rectal bleed' and was transferred to the intensive care unit.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: An expert testified regarding the average length of the anal canal and the length of the tip of the enema, stating that it could reach into the rectum and that it was possible for the tip to go through the rectum and cause the perforation.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: The trial court properly denied the defendant’s motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The trial court properly denied the motion to set aside the verdict, as the defendant could not prevail on its claim that the jury improperly was permitted to consider a theory of negligence unsupported by the evidence.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Bruce Cockayne developed a necrotizing infection and his health deteriorated precipitously due to sepsis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury awarded Bruce Cockayne $382,732.21 in past economic damages and $2,105,027.16 in noneconomic damages.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury awarded Marion Cockayne $720,000 for loss of consortium.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: An expert witness used differential diagnosis to eliminate three of the potential causes and opined that, to a reasonable degree of medical probability, an enema administered during B’s hospitalization caused the perforation.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

initial_state.chief_complaint: Fainting likely due to continued diarrhea and loss of fluids
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Bruce Cockayne was readmitted to the hospital after fainting, likely due to continued diarrhea and the resulting loss of fluids. He had previously been discharged on February 3, 2014, after treatment for diarrhea and vomiting.
medications: Rowasa enemas prescribed for home use

requestables.name: CT Scan
requestables.description: CT scan performed to assess internal bleeding

reveal.result_summary: CT scan revealed a large, older appearing perforation in the posterior right rectum
reveal.clinical_significance: Identified the source of the perforation

requestables.name: Endoscopy
requestables.description: Endoscopy performed to identify source of bleeding

reveal.result_summary: Endoscopy identified an internal hemorrhoid causing massive bleeding and a perforation in the rectum
reveal.clinical_significance: Confirmed the source of bleeding and perforation

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient readmitted to hospital after fainting

timeline_phases.name: Diagnostic Workup
timeline_phases.description: Patient undergoes diagnostic procedures to identify cause of symptoms

timeline_phases.name: Surgical Intervention
timeline_phases.description: Surgical procedures performed to address complications

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with fainting and diarrhea readmitted to hospital
decision_points.prompt: What is the appropriate method for administering the Rowasa enema?

options.description: Administer Rowasa enema with caution, ensuring proper technique
options.clinical_reasoning: Proper technique is necessary to avoid complications such as perforation

options.description: Administer Rowasa enema without specific caution
options.clinical_reasoning: Failure to use proper technique can lead to perforation

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant administered Rowasa enema without proper technique

expected_action_court.description: Rowasa enema should have been administered with proper technique
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated proper technique was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to use proper technique led to perforation
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Proper technique should have been used to prevent perforation

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed necrotizing infection and health deteriorated

legal_outcome.damages_awarded: 382732.21 in past economic damages and 2,105,027.16 in noneconomic damages
key_findings: Breach of duty in enema administration
Causation established - rectal perforation led to severe complications

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Proper procedure would have prevented perforation

treatments.outcome: Attempt to manage perforation and prevent further complications
